title: Problem 1 (Brass et al.)
statement_text: Is there a set $\mathcal{G} = \{G_1, G_2\}$ consisting of two planar graphs of the same order such that $\mathcal{G}$ is not simultaneously embeddable?
context_text: A set of planar graphs of the same order with no simultaneous embedding is called a conflict collection. This problem asks whether a conflict collection of the smallest conceivable size — just two graphs — can exist. It was posed in 2007 and remains widely open; the function $\sigma(n)$ denotes the smallest size of a conflict collection of $n$-vertex planar graphs, and Problem 1 is equivalent to asking whether $\sigma(n) = 2$ for some $n$.
attributed_to: Brass, Cenek, Duncan, Efrat, Erten, Ismailescu, Kobourov, Lubiw and Mitchell
paper_contribution: Proves that $\sigma(n) \leq (3+o(1))\log_2(n)$, a double-exponential improvement over the previous best bound of $O(n \cdot 4^{n/11})$, and gives a computer-free proof that a conflict collection of size 30 exists; the existence of a conflict collection of size 2 remains open.
paper_title: A logarithmic bound for simultaneous embeddings of planar graphs
